claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions. Let's start with the basics.

**How old is the child you're claiming for?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you're claiming for multiple children, we can go through each one - just let me know about the first child to start with.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 18 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm happy to help you check if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, who is 18 years old.

Since Alex is in the **16 to 19 age range**, eligibility depends on his current situation with education or training. Let me navigate to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For young people aged 16-19 like Alex, Child Benefit can continue if they meet certain conditions.

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current situation?**

1. **In education or training** - Alex is currently enrolled in and attending a qualifying course or approved training programme

2. **Recently left education or training** - Alex has recently finished or left education/training (within the last few months)

3. **Neither** - Alex is not in education/training and hasn't been recently (e.g., working full-time, unemployed but not in education)

Which applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. He finished school a while back and isn't studying anything right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex finished school "a while back" and isn't currently in any education or training, I need to check one more thing before we can determine eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. The extension period for Child Benefit after leaving education is **20 weeks**, and this only applies to young people up to age 18.

Just to confirm - when you say Alex "finished school a while back," can you give me a rough idea of how long ago that was? 

- Was it **more than 20 weeks ago** (roughly 5 months or more)?
- Or was it **within the last 20 weeks** (less than about 5 months ago)?

This is important because there's an extension period that can apply if a young person has recently left education and is registered with certain services.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, it was definitely more than 5 months ago - probably closer to a year now, I'd say.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex left education about a year ago, that's well beyond the 20-week extension period that could have applied.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Based on the information you've provided, I now have enough to give you a determination about Child Benefit eligibility for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Result for Alex

**Result: NOT ELIGIBLE** ❌

I'm sorry to say that based on the information you've provided, you are **not currently eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

### Why?

For young people aged 16-19, Child Benefit can only be claimed if they are:
- In **approved education** (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study), OR
- On an **approved training course**

Since Alex:
- Is 18 years old
- Is not currently in education or training
- Left school about a year ago (well past the 20-week extension period)

He doesn't meet the criteria for a "qualifying child" under Child Benefit rules.

### What are your options?

1. **If Alex returns to education** - If Alex decides to enrol in a qualifying course (such as A-levels, BTECs, T-levels, NVQs up to Level 3, or similar) for at least 12 hours per week, you could potentially claim Child Benefit again. This would be possible until he turns 20.

2. **Other support** - While Child Benefit may not be available, Alex may be entitled to other support depending on his circumstances, such as Universal Credit if he's looking for work.

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify, or do you have any other children you'd like me to assess?

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and is not currently in approved education or training. He left school approximately a year ago, which is well beyond the 20-week extension period that allows Child Benefit to continue for 16-17 year olds who have recently left education and registered with careers services. For young people aged 16-19, Child Benefit is only payable if they are enrolled in qualifying education or approved training for at least 12 hours per week. Since Alex does not meet these criteria, he is not a 'qualifying child' for Child Benefit purposes.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is inquiring about Child Benefit for their 18-year-old son Alex. Since Alex is not in approved education or training and left school approximately a year ago (well beyond the 20-week extension period), he does not meet the definition of a 'qualifying child' for Child Benefit purposes. Child Benefit for 16-19 year olds requires the young person to be in qualifying education or approved training.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.
